NETFLIX ANALYSIS¶

PROBLEM STATEMENT¶

With the rapid expansion of digital streaming platforms,Netflix has developed a vast library of movies and Tv shows across different countries,types and durations.However,understanding the patterns within this large dataset is essential to identify trends in content production,audience targeting and platform growth.

This project aims to analayse the Netflix dataset to explore the distribution and characteristics of content available on the platform.The study focuses on identifying patterns in content type(Movies v/s Tv show),release trends over the years,country wise contributions,rating classification and duration.

ABOUT THE DATASET¶

  • The dataset used in this project is the Netflix Dataset. It contains information about movies and TV shows on Netflix.There are about 8,807 records in the dataset.
  • It includes details like title, type, director, cast, country, release year, rating, duration, and genre.Some data was missing, so it was cleaned.
  • The duration column was also corrected. Movies are measured in minutes, and TV shows are measured in seasons.
  • This dataset helps us understand Netflix content, such as popular genres, release trends, and audience types. It is useful for learning data analysis.

AIM OF THE PROJECT¶

The aim of this dataset is to understand Netflix content. It helps to analyze movies and TV shows, find popular genres, study release year trends, and understand audience ratings. It is mainly used to learn data analysis and visualization.

DAY-1

Importing Libraries¶

In [1]:
import pandas as pd   #data handling
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt   #visualization
import seaborn as sns  #visualization

DAY-2

Loading Dataset¶

df=pd.read_csv("netflix.csv")
print(df)

DAY-3

Data Cleaning¶

Parse Date_added As Datetime¶

                                errors="coerce")

In [35]:
import pandas as pd

def categorize_rating(rating):
    if pd.isna(rating):
        return "Unknown"
    
    rating = str(rating).strip()

    # Handle wrong entries like durations
    if "min" in rating:
        return "Unknown"
    
    # Kids content
    if rating in ["TV-Y", "TV-Y7", "TV-Y7-FV", "G"]:
        return "Kids"
    
    # Family / General
    elif rating in ["TV-G", "PG", "TV-PG"]:
        return "Family"
    
    # Teens
    elif rating in ["PG-13", "TV-14"]:
        return "Teen"
    
    # Adults
    elif rating in ["R", "TV-MA", "NC-17"]:
        return "Adult"
    
    # Not Rated / Others
    elif rating in ["NR", "UR"]:
        return "Unrated"
    
    else:
        return "Unrated"
In [36]:
df["rating_category"] = df["rating"].apply(categorize_rating)
In [37]:
print(df["rating_category"].value_counts())

rating_category

Teen       2647
Family     1368
Kids        686
Unrated      82

How To Fill Missing Values¶

In [42]:
df['director'].fillna("unknown",inplace=True)

C:\Users\HP\AppData\Local\Temp\ipykernel_13860\2513338713.py:1: FutureWarning: A value is trying to be set on a copy of a DataFrame or Series through chained assignment using an inplace method.
The behavior will change in pandas 3.0. This inplace method will never work because the intermediate object on which we are setting values always behaves as a copy.

For example, when doing 'df[col].method(value, inplace=True)', try using 'df.method({col: value}, inplace=True)' or df[col] = df[col].method(value) instead, to perform the operation inplace on the original object.


  df['director'].fillna("unknown",inplace=True)
In [43]:
df['country'].fillna("unknown",inplace=True)

C:\Users\HP\AppData\Local\Temp\ipykernel_13860\367023652.py:1: FutureWarning: A value is trying to be set on a copy of a DataFrame or Series through chained assignment using an inplace method.
The behavior will change in pandas 3.0. This inplace method will never work because the intermediate object on which we are setting values always behaves as a copy.

For example, when doing 'df[col].method(value, inplace=True)', try using 'df.method({col: value}, inplace=True)' or df[col] = df[col].method(value) instead, to perform the operation inplace on the original object.


  df['country'].fillna("unknown",inplace=True)
In [44]:
df['cast'].fillna("unknown",inplace=True)

C:\Users\HP\AppData\Local\Temp\ipykernel_13860\2462058862.py:1: FutureWarning: A value is trying to be set on a copy of a DataFrame or Series through chained assignment using an inplace method.
The behavior will change in pandas 3.0. This inplace method will never work because the intermediate object on which we are setting values always behaves as a copy.

For example, when doing 'df[col].method(value, inplace=True)', try using 'df.method({col: value}, inplace=True)' or df[col] = df[col].method(value) instead, to perform the operation inplace on the original object.


  df['cast'].fillna("unknown",inplace=True)

Filtering The Data -movie and tv show¶

Parase Movies As Numerical¶

In [59]:
movies["duration"] = movies["duration"].str.replace("min"," ",regex=False)
movies["duration"] = pd.to_numeric(movies["duration"], errors="coerce")

C:\Users\HP\AppData\Local\Temp\ipykernel_13860\1304505552.py:1: SettingWithCopyWarning: 
A value is trying to be set on a copy of a slice from a DataFrame.
Try using .loc[row_indexer,col_indexer] = value instead

See the caveats in the documentation: https://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/stable/user_guide/indexing.html#returning-a-view-versus-a-copy

  • the data set contains 8790 entries
  • the average release year is 2014
  • the standard deviation is 8.825
  • the oldest content dates back to 1925
  • 25% of the content was released before 2013
  • The median release year is 2017
  • 75% of the content was released before 2019
  • The latest content is from 2021

top s1 Movie Dick Johnson Is Dead unknown unknown United States 2020-01-01 TV-MA 1 Season Dramas, International Movies Paranormal activity at a lush, abandoned prope... Adult
freq 1 6126 1 2621 825 2809 110 3205 1791 362 4 4007
  • total 8790 entries,means no missing values
  • 2 unique values are movie and tv shows,dataset is heavily movie-dominate
  • every titles are unique
  • 4527 directors are unique ,the most of them are unknown(2621)
  • 7679 cast are unique,most of them are unknown(825)
  • most content dominated by the united states
  • 110 shows were released on 01/01/2020
  • majority content TV-MA
  • drama & international movies are the majority of the listed_in
  • most target on adult audience

DAY-5

Data Visualization¶

Univariate Analysis¶

  • histogram,kde,box plot - numerical
  • count, pie - categorical

Bivariate Analysis¶

  • bar plot - category vs numerical
  • line plot(time series) and scatter - numerical vs numerical

Multivariate Analysis¶

  • heat map - correlation
  • pair plot - numerical

Univariate Analysis¶

Histogram¶

  • Distribution of continous numerical data
In [87]:
import seaborn as sns 
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
In [88]:
plt.figure(figsize=(4,6))      
sns.histplot(data=df_new,x="release_year",bins=50,color="purple",kde=True)
plt.title("Distribution of Release Year")
plt.xlabel("Release Year")
plt.show()
  • The histogram shows the distribution of release year
  • A huge majority of the releases happened in the last 10-15 years.
  • There are very few releases from before the year 2000.
  • The no.of releases started growing very fast after 2010.
  • 2018 and 2019 were the biggest year of releases.
  • The numbers drop suddenly after 2019
  • The distribution is strongly left-skewed
  • Most of the data is crowded on the right side (recent years)
In [89]:
plt.figure(figsize=(4,6))      
sns.histplot(data=df_new1,x="release_year",bins=50,color="purple",kde=True)
plt.title("Distribution of Release Year(Movies)")
plt.xlabel("Release Year")
plt.show()
  • Most content is released after 2000
  • Majority of release happened in 10-15 years
  • Releases from 1940 to 1980 are very rare
  • There is a massive spike right around 2018
  • After 2018 peak,there is a quick decline in the no.of releases in leading into 2020
In [90]:
plt.figure(figsize=(5,6))      
sns.histplot(data=df_new2,x="release_year",bins=50,color="purple",kde=True)
plt.title("Distribution of Release Year(TV_shows)")
plt.xlabel("Release Year")
plt.show()
  • Almost all tv shows were released after 2010
  • TV shows from 1940 to 2000 are extremely rare
  • 2019 has the most releases followed by a small drop in 2020
  • Majority of release happened in last 5-10 years

Box Plot¶

In [91]:
plt.figure(figsize=(5,4))   
sns.boxplot(data=df_new,x="release_year",color="purple")
plt.title("Distribution of Release Year")
plt.xlabel("Release Year")
plt.show()
  • 50% of all releases happened between approximately 2013 and 2019
  • The vertical line in the box shows the middle value is around 2017
  • The long trail of circles to the left shows that releases before 2005 are rare and considered outliers
In [92]:
plt.figure(figsize=(5,4))   
sns.boxplot(data=df_new1,x="release_year",color="purple")
plt.title("Distribution of Release Year")
plt.xlabel("Release Year")
plt.show()
  • Most data is concentrated in 2010–2020
  • The distribution is skewed toward recent years
  • Many older years appear as outliers
  • The median is in recent years
  • Very few entries before 2000
In [93]:
plt.figure(figsize=(5,4))   
sns.boxplot(data=df_new2,x="release_year",color="purple")
plt.title("Distribution of Release Year")
plt.xlabel("Release Year")
plt.show()
  • Most titles are recent: the catalog is heavily weighted toward releases after 2010.
  • Movies dominate the dataset over TV shows.
  • Adult-rated content is the largest rating group.
  • Netflix content peaks around 2018–2019 and drops after 2019.
  • TV shows are usually short-run (1–3 seasons) in this dataset.
  • Older releases (pre-2000) are rare and appear as outliers.

Count Plot¶

In [94]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))
sns.countplot(data=df_new,x="release_year",color="purple")
plt.title("Distribution of Release Year")
plt.xlabel("Release Year")
plt.show()
  • Most releaes are after 2010
  • there is only very few releases before 2000
  • Release increases quickly after 2010
  • The highest releases count are in 2018–2019
  • After 2019, counts drop a bit
  • The data is right-heavy, meaning recent years dominate
In [95]:
plt.figure(figsize=(5,4))   
sns.countplot(data=df_new1,x="release_year",color="purple")
plt.title("Distribution of Release Year(Movies)")
plt.xlabel("Release Year")
plt.show()
  • Most titles are from 2010 onward.
  • Movies far outnumber TV shows.
  • Adult-rated content is the largest group.
  • Release volume peaks around 2018–2019.
In [96]:
plt.figure(figsize=(5,6))   
sns.countplot(data=df_new2,x="release_year",color="purple")
plt.title("Distribution of Release Year(TV_shows)")
plt.xlabel("Release Year")
plt.show()
  • Most catalog entries are recent (after 2010).
  • Movies dominate over TV shows.
  • Adult-rated content is the largest group.

Pie Chart¶

In [97]:
x=df_new['rating_category'].value_counts()
labels=['Adult','Teen','Family','Kids','Underated']
plt.figure(figsize=(5,4))
plt.pie(x,labels=labels,autopct='%1.1f%%')  
plt.title("Distribution of Rating category")
plt.xlabel("Rating category")
plt.show()
  • This pie chart shows how many titles are in each rating group.
  • Adult is the biggest group, so most titles are for mature viewers.
  • Teen is the next biggest group.
  • Family and Kids are smaller, so child-friendly content is less common.
  • Unrated titles are very few.
In [98]:
x=df_new1['rating_category'].value_counts()
labels=['Adult','Teen','Family','Kids','Underated']
plt.figure(figsize=(5,4))
plt.pie(x,labels=labels,autopct='%1.1f%%')  
plt.title("Distribution of Rating category")
plt.xlabel("Rating category")
plt.show()
In [99]:
x=df_new2['rating_category'].value_counts()
labels=['Adult','Teen','Family','Kids','Underated']
plt.figure(figsize=(5,4))
plt.pie(x,labels=labels,autopct='%1.1f%%')  
plt.title("Distribution of Rating category")
plt.xlabel("Rating category")
plt.show()

Bivariate Analysis¶

Bar Plot¶

In [100]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10,5))   
sns.barplot(data=df_new1,x="rating_category",y="duration",color="purple")
plt.title("Distribution of Rating category")
plt.xlabel("Rating category")
plt.show()
  • The bar plot shows average length for each rating group.
  • Teen and Adult categories have the longest durations.
  • Kids content is much shorter than other groups.

Line Plot¶

In [101]:
plt.figure(figsize=(5,4))   
sns.lineplot(data=df_new1,x="release_year",y=("duration"))
plt.title("Distribution of Release Year")
plt.xlabel("Release Year")
plt.show()
  • Movie lengths increased sharply from the 1940s to the mid-1960s
  • Peak average duration occurred around the 1960s
  • High variability in movie lengths during the 1960s–1980s
  • Gradual decline in duration after the 1970s
  • Modern films (2000s onward) are shorter and more consistent
  • Overall trend: rise → peak → steady decline → stabilization
In [102]:
plt.figure(figsize=(5,4))   
sns.lineplot(data=df_new2,x="release_year",y=("duration"))
plt.title("Distribution of Release Year")
plt.xlabel("Rellease Year")
plt.show()
  • Values stay very low and flat until around the 1950s
  • Sharp spikes appear between the 1960s–1980s (high instability)
  • Peak occurs around the late 1970s–early 1980s
  • After 1990, values drop and fluctuate frequently
  • From 2000 onward, values stabilize at low levels
  • Overall trend: flat → volatile spikes → decline → low, stable pattern

Scatter Plot¶

In [103]:
plt.figure(figsize=(5,6))   
sns.scatterplot(data=df_new1,x="release_year",y=("duration"))
plt.title("Distribution of Release Year with duration")
plt.xlabel("Release Year")
plt.show()
  • Most movies cluster between ~80–150 minutes
  • After 2000, many more movies are released (denser points)
  • Wide spread in durations, but majority stay in mid-range
  • Some outliers: very short (<50 min) and very long (>200 min) films
  • Older films (pre-1970) are fewer but often longer
  • Overall: high variation, but typical movie length stays around ~100–140 minutes
In [104]:
plt.figure(figsize=(5,6))   
sns.scatterplot(data=df_new2,x="release_year",y=("duration"))
plt.title("Distribution of Release Year with duration")
plt.xlabel("Release Year")
plt.show()
  • Most TV shows are of smaller duration(1-3 seasons)
  • The number of releases increases very much after 2010.
  • Very long-running shows (duration above 10) are rare and appear as outliers.
  • The plot suggests Netflix content is skewed toward newer, shorter-run TV = - shows rather than long multi-season series.

DAY-6

Multivariate Analysis¶

HeatMap¶

In [105]:
df_new1_num=df_new1.select_dtypes(include='number')
df_new1_num.corr()
Out[105]:
release_year duration
release_year 1.000000 -0.206248
duration -0.206248 1.000000
In [106]:
plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))
sns.heatmap(df_new1.corr(numeric_only=True), annot=True, cmap="coolwarm")
plt.title("Correlation Heatmap")
plt.show()
  • The correlation between release_year and duration is -0.21.
  • This is a weak negative relationship → as release year increases, duration tends to slightly decrease.
  • The value is close to 0, so the relationship is not strong.
  • This means newer content is only slightly shorter on average, but not by much.
  • There is no strong pattern between release year and duration.
In [107]:
plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))
sns.heatmap(df_new2.corr(numeric_only=True),annot=True, cmap="coolwarm")
plt.title("Correlation Heatmap")
plt.show()

Pair Plot¶

In [108]:

sns.pairplot(df_new1.select_dtypes(include='number'), palette='coolwarm')

<seaborn.axisgrid.PairGrid at 0x2341832acf0>
<Figure size 600x400 with 0 Axes>
  • The pairplot shows no strong linear relationship between release_year and duration.
  • Points are scattered widely, so newer movies are not consistently longer or shorter.
  • Most movies cluster around typical durations, regardless of release year.
  • The trend is almost flat, confirming the weak negative correlation.
In [109]:
plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))
sns.pairplot(df_new2.select_dtypes(include='number'), palette='coolwarm')

  • most releases are from 2010 onward.
  • peak release activity is around 2018–2019.
  • Movies dominate the dataset.TV shows are fewer and mostly short-run.
  • Ratings are skewed toward adult content, with teen content second-largest.
  • TV show durations are mostly 1–3 seasons; long series are rare.
  • Release year vs duration shows only a weak negative correlation.

DAY-7

CONCLUSION¶

In this project, the Netflix Dataset was successfully cleaned and analyzed. Several changes were made to improve the quality of the data. Missing values in columns like director, cast, and country were handled by filling or removing them.The movies and tv shows are seperated to a file. The duration column was cleaned and converted into a proper numeric format, where movies are shown in minutes and TV shows in seasons.

After cleaning, the dataset became more accurate and easy to use for analysis. The analysis showed that Netflix has more movies than TV shows, most content is released after 2010, and popular genres include drama and comedy. It also showed that content is mainly made for young and adult audiences.

Overall, this project helped in understanding how to clean data, fix errors, and perform data analysis. It shows the importance of data preprocessing before doing any analysis.

KEY INSIGHTS¶

  • Movies dominated in netflix than tv shows.
  • In the dataset 6126 Moviesand 2664 Tv shows are there.
  • Most content dominated by United States.
  • Most of the movies and tv shows target on adult audience.
  • After cleaning the dataset ,have 8790 entries.
  • The average release year is 2014.
  • The median release year is 2017.
  • The standard deviation of release year is 8.825.
  • Most of the directors are unknown.
  • Most of the cast are unknown.
  • Most of genre are drama & international movies.
  • A huge majority of the releases happened in the last 10-15 years.
  • The no.of releases started growing very fast after 2010.
  • After 2018 peak,there is a quick decline in the no.of releases in leading into 2020.
  • long trail of circles to the left shows that releases before 2005 are rare and considered outliers.
  • The highest releases count are in 2018-2019.
  • Adult is biggest rating category,so most titles are mature viewers.
  • Teen and adult categories have the longest durations.
  • Kids content is much shorter than other group.
  • Movie length increased sharply from the 1940's to the mid 1960's.
  • Some outliers very short (<50 min) and very long (>200 min) films.
  • The correlation between release year and duration is -0.21.
  • The value is close to 0,so the relationship is not strong
  • No strong linear relationship between release year and duration
  • Tv show duration are mostly 1-3 seasons
